title: Lancashire hotpot
subtitle: Lamb, onion, and sliced potato baked slow and uncovered
excerpt: Lamb neck chops and onion layered with thinly sliced potato, covered in stock, and baked slowly until the potato lid is golden and crisp and the lamb beneath is completely tender. No lid on the pot: the potatoes crispen on top while the lamb braises underneath. Red cabbage pickled in vinegar is the traditional accompaniment.
sourceNotes: Lancashire hotpot is a dish from the textile-working towns of northwest England, where cheap cuts of mutton or lamb were cooked slowly while workers were at the mills. The tall pottery pot that gave the dish its name is rare now — a standard casserole dish produces the same result. The dish is recognised as a regional specialty and appears on menus as a marker of Northern English cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble and refrigerate unbaked for up to 24 hours. Add 10 minutes to the baking time.
recipe.temperatureNote: 140°C fan / Gas 3, then 200°C / 180°C fan for the potato lid

content.text: Lamb neck chops are the traditional cut: the neck has good fat distribution and enough collagen to survive a 2-hour braise without drying out. Shoulder chops are a good alternative. Avoid leg — it will become dry.

The potato lid is overlapping slices, not a mash topping. The slices should be 3 to 4 mm thick — thin enough to cook through from the top without the lamb beneath overcooking, thick enough to hold their structure. A mandoline makes this faster.

Heat the oven to 160°C. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large casserole or frying pan over high heat. Season the {{lamb-neck}} with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}} and brown in batches, 2 minutes per side. Remove and set aside. In the same pan, cook the {{onion}} over medium heat for 5 minutes until softened. Layer the lamb and onion in a deep casserole dish. Add the {{bay-leaves}}, {{thyme}}, and {{worcestershire-sauce}}. Pour in the {{stock-beef}}. Arrange the {{potato}} slices in overlapping layers on top, seasoning each layer. Dot with {{unsalted-butter}}. Bake covered with foil at 160°C for 1 hour 30 minutes. Remove the foil, increase to 200°C, and bake for a further 30 minutes until the potato lid is golden and crisp.

items.symptom: Potato lid not crisping even after 30 minutes at high heat
items.cause: Too much liquid, or potatoes overlapping too thickly
items.fix: Ensure the stock does not come above the potato lid. If flooded, remove the lid and let it reduce before applying the potato slices

items.cause: Not enough collagen in the cut, or temperature too low
items.fix: Check the oven is reaching 160°C. Neck and shoulder need 2 hours to break down fully. If still tough, cover and continue at 160°C for another 30 minutes

content.text: Lancashire hotpot is a winter dish and a marker of Northern English regional cooking. It appears on pub menus across the North of England, always with pickled red cabbage. The long baking time and minimal preparation make it a practical dish for a cook who wants to leave it in the oven unattended.
